message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus (SARS-CoV)-2 is spreading rapidly in the absence of validated tools to control the growing epidemic besides social distancing and masks. Many efforts are ongoing for the development of vaccines against SARS-CoV-2 since there is an imminent need to develop effective interventions for controlling and preventing SARS-CoV-2 spread. Essentially all vaccines in most advanced phases are based on the induction of antibody response against either whole or part of spike (S) protein. Differently, we developed an original strategy to induce CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cytotoxic lymphocyte (CTL) immunity based on
                  <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                  engineering of extracellular vesicles (EVs). We exploited this technology with the aim to identify a clinical candidate defined as DNA vectors expressing SARS-CoV-2 antigens inducing a robust CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cell response. This is a new vaccination approach employing a DNA expression vector encoding a biologically inactive HIV-1 Nef protein (Nef
                  <jats:sup>mut</jats:sup>
                  ) showing an unusually high efficiency of incorporation into EVs even when foreign polypeptides are fused to its C-terminus. Nanovesicles containing Nef
                  <jats:sup>mut</jats:sup>
                  -fused antigens released by muscle cells are internalized by antigen-presenting cells leading to cross-presentation of the associated antigens thereby priming of antigen-specific CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T-cells. To apply this technology to a design of anti-SARS-CoV-2 vaccine, we recovered DNA vectors expressing the products of fusion between Nef
                  <jats:sup>mut</jats:sup>
                  and four viral antigens, namely N- and C-terminal moieties of S (referred to as S1 and S2), M, and N. All fusion products are efficiently uploaded in EVs. When the respective DNA vectors were injected in mice, a strong antigen-specific CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cell immunity was generated. Most important, high levels of virus-specific CD8
                  <jats:sup>+</jats:sup>
                  T cells were found in bronchoalveolar lavages of immunized mice. Co-injection of DNA vectors expressing the diverse SARS-CoV-2 antigens resulted in additive immune responses in both spleen and lung. EVs engineered with SARS-CoV-2 antigens proved immunogenic also in the human system through cross-priming assays carried out with
                  <jats:italic>ex vivo</jats:italic>
                  human cells. Hence, DNA vectors expressing Nef
                  <jats:sup>mut</jats:sup>
                  -based fusion proteins can be proposed as anti-SARS-CoV-2 vaccine candidates.
                </jats:p>
title:
A new concept on anti-SARS-CoV-2 vaccines: strong CD8
                  <sup>+</sup>
                  T-cell immune response in both spleen and lung induced in mice by endogenously engineered extracellular vesicles
